preFix.title: Ruling Up Guidelines
preFix.subtitle: Setting accurate writing lines with pencil, light box, and nib-width staircase
preFix.excerpt: Consistent letterforms depend on consistent line spacing. This technique covers three methods for ruling up a practice sheet or finished page: the H pencil method, the light-box guide-sheet method, and the nib-width staircase for calculating x-height from scratch.
preFix.sourceNotes: Johnston, Edward. Writing and Illuminating and Lettering. London: John Hogg, 1906. Chapter on ruling and page preparation.

content.text: Why ruling matters

Letterforms that sit at the right height look settled and authoritative. Letterforms that drift above or below the line look uncertain, even when the stroke itself is well made. Ruling up before you write is not a preliminary chore: it is the first act of the finished piece.
There are three practical methods. The pencil method is the most direct and suits small runs of finished work. The light-box method with a
guide sheet
keeps the finished surface clean. The
nib-width staircase
is not a ruling method in itself but a calculation tool that tells you where the lines should go before you draw them.
The nib-width staircase
Every calligraphic script has a specified
x-height
in nib widths. Foundational Hand is four nib widths. Italic is three and a half to four and a half. Uncial is four to five. The staircase turns those numbers into a measurement you can rule from.

Hold the pen at 0 degrees, perfectly flat, edge to the paper. Make a short horizontal stroke. Directly below it, touching but not overlapping, make a second. Keep going until you have the right number of strokes stacked. The distance from the bottom of the lowest stroke to the top of the highest is your x-height for that nib. Mark this at the left margin, then use the ruler to draw the lines across the page.

Ascender and descender space is added above and below the x-height band. For Foundational Hand, both are typically another two to three nib widths. Interline space (the gap between the descender line of one row and the ascender line of the next) is a matter of taste; Johnston recommended a minimum equal to the x-height, which prevents clashing ascenders and descenders from adjacent lines.
The H pencil method

An H or 2H pencil leaves a fine, light line that erases cleanly after the ink has dried. A softer pencil deposits too much graphite; the line may smear under a broadnib and become visible through the ink.
Use the nib-width staircase on a scrap of the same paper to find your x-height measurement and note it in millimetres.
Mark the baseline of the first writing line about 25 mm from the top of the sheet. Tick lightly at both the left and right margins.
From the baseline, measure upward by the x-height and tick. This is the waist line.
Measure further upward for the ascender line and downward from the baseline for the descender line.
Repeat the pattern for each subsequent writing line, leaving the chosen interline gap.
Connect the ticks along each line with a single, light stroke against the metal ruler. Apply no more pressure than the pencil's own weight.
After writing, allow the ink to dry for at least 20 minutes before erasing. A soft vinyl eraser removes the pencil cleanly without abrading the ink.
The light-box guide-sheet method

A guide sheet is ruled once, reused indefinitely, and never touches the finished piece. Because the writing paper rests on top, there is no graphite to erase and no risk of over-pressing the pencil into a surface that will hold ink.

Rule the guide sheet carefully on 130 gsm cartridge paper using the H pencil method above. Use a fine-tipped pen for the lines if you want a guide sheet you will re-use for multiple sessions, since pencil lines can smear under repeated sheets. Mark the script name and nib size on the guide sheet so it can be retrieved without measuring again.

To use: place the guide sheet on the light box, lay the writing paper over it, and switch the box on. The lines will read through papers up to about 100 gsm without difficulty. Heavier papers may need the box on its highest setting. If the guide sheet lines are still faint through thick paper, go over them with a broad black pen to thicken them.
Practical notes
Rule the baseline and waist line first. Ascender and descender lines matter less on shorter practice words and can be added after.
Lines should be barely visible at arm's length. If they are clearly visible they are too dark.

The metal ruler rather than a set square gives more accurate horizontals on a sheet that is not quite square to the table edge. Line up the ruler against a printed or carefully measured vertical at the side margin.
On vellum or very thick paper the light-box method won't work, the lines won't show through. Draw the lines in pencil instead and erase them before you ink.
